title: PipeER: Error Amplification in Entity Resolution Pipelines -- A Formal Analysis of Stage-Wise Quality Propagation
description: We propose PipeER, a formal framework for modeling how errors propagate and amplify across the stages of an entity resolution pipeline (blocking, matching, clustering). We derive closed-form expressions showing that end-to-end recall is bounded by blocking recall (irrecoverable errors), define Error Amplification Factors that identify the pipeline bottleneck, and provide a stage-optimal allocation algorithm for improvement effort.
motivation: Current ER research evaluates pipeline stages in isolation -- blocking recall, matching F1, and clustering metrics are reported separately. This creates a blind spot: practitioners cannot determine whether to invest in better blocking, matching, or clustering to maximize end-to-end quality. Errors at different stages have fundamentally asymmetric propagation behavior (blocking errors are irrecoverable while matching errors can be partially recovered by clustering), but this asymmetry has never been formally modeled.
proposed_approach: PipeER has three components: (1) An Error Propagation Model (EPM) that derives end-to-end precision, recall, and F1 as closed-form functions of per-stage quality metrics, proving R_e2e <= PC (blocking recall bounds end-to-end recall); (2) Error Amplification Factors (EAFs) -- per-stage sensitivity coefficients analogous to Amdahl's Law -- that identify the pipeline bottleneck where improvement yields the greatest end-to-end gain; (3) A Stage-Optimal Allocation (SOA) algorithm that optimally distributes improvement effort across stages under budget constraints. The framework is validated empirically via controlled stage degradation experiments on 6 benchmark datasets with 4 matching methods, 3 blocking methods, and 3 clustering methods.

related_work.title: An Overview of End-to-End Entity Resolution for Big Data
related_work.authors: Vassilis Christophides, Vasilis Efthymiou, Themis Palpanas, George Papadakis, Kostas Stefanidis
related_work.relation: Comprehensive survey covering all ER pipeline stages independently. Does not formally model how per-stage errors compound into end-to-end quality. PipeER fills this gap with closed-form propagation equations.

related_work.title: (Almost) All of Entity Resolution
related_work.relation: Unified statistical treatment of ER emphasizing uncertainty propagation. Motivates propagating uncertainty through all stages but does not provide formal error amplification analysis or optimization. PipeER operationalizes this insight with quantitative EAFs.

related_work.title: Blocking and Filtering Techniques for Entity Resolution: A Survey
related_work.authors: George Papadakis, Dimitrios Skoutas, Emmanouil Thanos, Themis Palpanas
related_work.relation: Standard reference on blocking evaluation metrics (pairs completeness, reduction ratio). Evaluates blocking in isolation. PipeER extends this by formally quantifying blocking's downstream impact on matching and clustering.

related_work.title: Framework for Evaluating Clustering Algorithms in Duplicate Detection
related_work.authors: Oktie Hassanzadeh, Fei Chiang, Hyun Chul Lee, Renee J. Miller
related_work.relation: Defines cluster-level evaluation metrics for ER. PipeER builds on these metrics but places clustering within the full pipeline, analyzing how matching errors shape clustering behavior.

related_work.title: A Critical Re-evaluation of Benchmark Datasets for (Deep) Learning-Based Matching Algorithms
related_work.authors: George Papadakis, Nishadi Kirielle, Peter Christen, Themis Palpanas
related_work.relation: Characterizes dataset difficulty using complexity measures. PipeER complements this by showing that the bottleneck stage depends on dataset difficulty -- easy datasets bottleneck at matching, hard datasets at blocking.

related_work.title: SAGA: A Scalable Framework for Optimizing Data Cleaning Pipelines for Machine Learning Applications
related_work.authors: Shafaq Siddiqi, Roman Kern, Matthias Boehm
related_work.relation: Black-box pipeline optimization for ML tasks. PipeER provides a white-box formal model specific to ER pipelines, enabling analytical bottleneck identification without expensive search.

related_work.title: Magellan: Toward Building Entity Matching Management Systems
related_work.authors: Pradap Konda, Sanjib Das, AnHai Doan, et al.
related_work.relation: Foundational ER management system. Used as one of the matching methods in our experimental evaluation.

related_work.title: A Theory for Record Linkage
related_work.authors: Ivan P. Fellegi, Alan B. Sunter
related_work.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association, 64(328), 1969
related_work.relation: Foundational probabilistic model for single-stage record linkage decisions. PipeER extends the analysis to the full multi-stage pipeline.

related_work.title: Deep Learning for Entity Matching: A Design Space Exploration
related_work.authors: Sidharth Mudgal, Han Li, Theodoros Rekatsinas, AnHai Doan, et al.
related_work.relation: Key DL-based ER method. PipeER is method-agnostic and studies how matching-stage improvements from DL translate to end-to-end gains.

related_work.title: Deep Entity Matching with Pre-Trained Language Models
related_work.authors: Yuliang Li, Jinfeng Li, Yoshihiko Suhara, AnHai Doan, Wang-Chiew Tan
related_work.relation: State-of-the-art PLM-based ER. PipeER analyzes whether the end-to-end gains from such matching improvements are limited by blocking or clustering bottlenecks.

related_work.title: Efficient and Effective ER with Progressive Blocking
related_work.authors: Sainyam Galhotra, Donatella Firmani, Barna Saha, Divesh Srivastava
related_work.relation: Recognizes blocking-matching interaction by using matching output to refine blocking. PipeER provides the formal framework that explains why such interaction is valuable.

hypothesis: End-to-end ER quality is a non-linear function of per-stage quality metrics, with blocking recall as a hard upper bound on end-to-end recall. A formal Error Propagation Model can predict end-to-end F1 from per-stage metrics with R^2 >= 0.85, and the Stage-Optimal Allocation algorithm achieves 1.5-3x higher F1 improvement per unit effort compared to uniform allocation across pipeline stages.

primary: EPM predicts end-to-end F1 with R^2 >= 0.85 across all datasets and configurations
Error Amplification Factors differ by at least 2x between highest and lowest stages on >= 4/6 datasets
SOA allocation outperforms uniform allocation by >= 20% in F1 improvement per unit effort on >= 4/6 datasets
secondary: Hard recall bound R_e2e <= PC empirically confirmed on all datasets
Bottleneck stage predictably shifts based on dataset difficulty (easy -> matching bottleneck, hard -> blocking bottleneck)
Analytical and empirical EAFs agree within 15% relative error
negative: Per-stage qualities are approximately independent (errors don't compound across stages)
EPM fails to predict end-to-end quality (R^2 < 0.5)
EAFs are approximately equal across stages (no bottleneck structure exists)
